sentences.text: Yes.
As I said, we would come from work tired, exhausted, hungry, cold.
We would be called to [NON-ENGLISH], roll call, where we would be forced to stand outdoors in all kinds of weather, and wonder what our Nazi guards were doing to us.
When it was snowing, they would tell us to lay flat in the snow and crawl.
We had no warm clothing on.
Do you know what happens to hands, to knees, when you lay in the snow?
Or when it poured, they told us to make long lines all the way from one end to the other.
And they told us to either use little pails or drinking cups, scoop up water from each puddle, pass it down the line, and pour it beyond the fence.
Do you know what happens to a puddle of water when it rains?
You scoop out the water and it fills up again.
Our passport to life really was work.
As long as you could produce, you were allowed to live.
The minute you became too weak or too sick, you were done away with.
But this no longer was work to produce, this was work to humiliate and degrade us.
From time to time, they would tell us to remove our uniform and stand there in the nude.
They called it bodily inspection.
I don't believe that the guards-- the main guards in particular-- did this to look at us females.
Because, by that time, we resembled anything but females.
There was nothing left on our bodies but skin and bone.
I don't think that we could appeal to a man as a female.
But just think what it did to our own person, standing there and viewing each other totally naked, totally helpless, totally powerless, so degraded.
What did they did do to us?
It was at times such as these that you sometimes begin feeling that you are indeed an untermensch, subhumans.
So many times they succeeded in making us feel as an untermensch.
Punishments during the roll call, such as beating or shaving your hair, was already an everyday occurrence.
It had almost no effect on us.
But it was what they did to us that mentally affected us was sometimes even worse than just beating.
And this could happen any time, or many times we'd come from work and a girl would be called to barrack duty after working so hard all day.
And if the girl was too weak, or too sick, another girl who was not much stronger would go and do it for her.
Barrack duty, like go and wash the toilets or the washrooms.
Toilets, our toilets were-- it was on a hill on the side of the camp.
It was just a little building with a board and holes in it.
There was no toilet such as we're thinking toilets that you flush it or you sit.
So you had to wash that or washroom.
Our washroom was also a long hall with like a long sink and just a pipe going across with water dipping out of it.
This was the washroom.
Or washing floors, or sweeping.

If a girl would be called to barrack duty such as that, and if she was too weak, and, as I said, another girl would go and do it for her, so as to cover up that she's unable, that she's too sick, to give her time to perhaps let her live.
I cannot describe to you how helpful-- how we covered up for each other.
This period of wanting to live, of helping each other, I think that this was such a tremendous factor in our survival.
That we didn't let each other fall, that we gave each other courage, that we did sing songs from time to time.
All that was effective and really helped us an awful lot.
Well, from Poland, I came with the last transport to the concentration camp.
Not until 1944, when the Hungarian girls arrived in our camp, it was then-- and they-- most of them went through Auschwitz.
And it was then in '44 that we realized-- that we learned-- that there is such a place as Auschwitz.
These girls came.
They where-- they looked different than we did.
Their hair was shaved completely, ours was not.
Ours was cut very short.
But if they punished you, they would shave a cross on your head, and they called it [NON-ENGLISH], the street of lies.
They would punish you in such a manner for- in the factory where we worked, there were POWs working, British and Russians.
And if as much as one of them looked at you, that's what they did to your hair.
And they would say, now let's see if they look at you again.
I don't even think that-- they just looked at us.
They tried to give a smile just to give us a little courage.
It was-- there could be no relationship or talk among us at all.
Because, in the factory, as well as in the barracks, we were guarded by Nazi guards all the time.
But when they arrived, they were still fresh.
They were still looked like females.
And it was then that we realized what a horrible sight we are.
And they realized it too.
But we ourselves-- you know, this was a slow degradation.
And we sort of like got used to looking the way we did.
There was a girl in our stube, in our room, who had epilepsy.
And for 2 and 1/2 years, we managed to hide it from the Nazis.
But how sad that one month before the war had ended, she had a seizure during one of the cell appels and was removed, and we never saw her again.
How sad it was that only one month later, the war has ended.
You know, when they won, they gave us soap-- new soap.
But not often we had soap.
And the soap we called it the RIF.
It had the initials R-I-F, which they told us stood for Rein Judisches Fett, Pure Jewish Fat.
I don't know until today if that soap was actually made of human fat or not.
But to give pain to the injury that we had already, they told us that it was made of Jewish fat.
And as I say, I don't know if it was made or not, but telling us about it-- how we felt?
At first, we did not want to use that soap.
But, you know, the will to live persists.
It's the strongest of all.
And wanting to live, wanting to keep clean, not because cleanliness was all that important to us, but there were all kinds of diseases raging throughout the camp, typhus, and all that.
And also a disease that settled between your fingers, [NON-ENGLISH], they called it.
And wanting to be clean for the sake of living, we would scrape the initials and eventually began using the soap, having no other choice.
And, again, this added more pain to us.
Shortly before the war has ended, our factory was closed.
Of course, we did not know why.
We didn't realize that the Allies were approaching.
And the supplies to our factory could not be shipped.
Therefore, slowly, they were closing up different sections of the factory.
What happened then, we were in the spring of 1945, we were forced to work in the fields building trenches.
But also working in fields, tilling the soil, and stuff like that.
It was then that my sister became very ill.
She had-- my sister, Mania, she had typhoid.
Typhoid certainly meant death, for there was no medication to be gotten.
There was a sick room.
And by that time, there was a Russian doctor in our camp.
She was one of the Russian prisoners of war caught by the Nazis.
And since they would not place her in a men's camp, they brought her to our camp.
But what could she do with two bare hands?
Nothing.
She had no medication.
Nothing.
My sister was taken to the sick room.
And the nurse in the sick room was a girl from my hometown.
After she was there a couple days, she told her and several other girls, tomorrow, you're going to work.
That was when we still worked in the factory at the end.
And in the morning, she dressed several of the girls and actually pushed them out into the hall, and they went to work.
My sister could not perform.
She lay in the back of the machine like a dead dog.
And the girl--
I wasn't in the same building.
No.
But the girl who worked the machine next to hers was helping her, working the machines for her.
And whenever she saw the Nazi guard approaching, she would help her to get up on her feet, lean on the machine so she could at least pretend she is working.
We were angry at the nurse, who was a girl, one of us, from our hometown, whom we knew.
Why would she do that?
But when we returned that day from the factory, we knew why.
She either heard something, overheard something, knew something.
And that's why she pushed those that could barely stand on their feet out of the sick room.
For that day, while we were at work, the Gestapo came, took all the sick girls from the sick room, shipped them out of our camp, and we never saw them again.
So by doing what she did, she saved the lives of the few girls that she helped, and she made them go to work that day.
And we first were so angry at her, not knowing.
My sister survived.
We didn't realize then that she sacrificed her life, that girl.
Because had the Nazis known what she did, she would too have been taken away.
So there were all kinds of acts of heroism in a way.
This was heroic.
But trying just to live and survive, I think, is the greatest act of heroism under the condition that we lived.
Wanting to live, wanting to survive, is, I think, heroism.
Because it would have been so easy to give up, and to die, and to perish, and to stop suffering so much, yet, I, for example, never gave up.
And most of us never.
Because, as I said, once you give up, there was nothing to sustain you and you perished.
I lived in that camp for two years, not knowing how the war was progressing.
For, as I said, we were totally, totally cut off from the rest of the world.
Out of the 2,000 girls that worked in our factory, there was only one escape.
Had the girl that managed to escape-- I don't know if she survived the escape.
Had she known what will happen to us, what punishment we would have to endure, I don't think she would have made an attempt.
In our factory, there were Czech citizens working in the factory, for Ober Altstadt was a part of Germany that actually belonged to Czechoslovakia at one time.
And there were a lot of Czech people living in that section of Germany.
And, obviously, some who worked in the factory were also in the Czech underground.
And, somehow, they managed to smuggle her out of the factory.

But what happened, our [NON-ENGLISH], the head of our concentration camp, was a woman, who, before the war, was just factory worker, whose power was the growth of the camp, became more vicious, whose name also happened to be Irma Hoffmann.
And the girl who managed to escape was also named Hoffmann.

And we don't know if it was that she feared that somebody might accuse her of having something to do, or because she was angry that one of us could manage to escape, I cannot tell you of the punishment we had to endure.
No food, and, of course, cell appel without an end.
We just stood there until we were collapsing.
I think that had the girl known what will happen because of her escape, I don't think she would have escaped.
We didn't find out after the war whether she was alive or not.
I hope she was.
Anyway, we worked in the factory.
There were POWs working, British, as well as Russian.
They lived in different camps, not under the conditions that we did.
But, nevertheless, they were prisoners.
There was one British-- or rather South African prisoner that-- I will never forget his name-- he worked across from me in a building that his window was facing mine.
And somehow, he tried to signal to me through the window that he-- I am trying to send you something to eat.
And through a French prisoner of war, or there were French men working in the factory too, and they brought the supplies to my machines.

And through that man, he sent me a tiny little, tiny square, of chocolate, which I haven't had chocolate for so many years, and also a picture of his little sister, which I have until today, in a scout uniform.
And he wrote that I remind him so much of his little sister.
I remember his name until today.
After the war, I tried to contact him.
I don't know if he survived because the winters were brutal for the soldiers from Africa.
His name was George C.
Hays.
And his address was P-O-U-G-I-E, South Africa.
I can never forget his name for that tiny bit of chocolate that probably didn't mean so much to anyone.

But to know that the man thought of me and wanted to send it to me, I could never forget someone who opened his heart, who thought that there was a little girl and I am going to give her something that she never had for many, many years.
So it was acts like this that you never forget.
I would have loved to have-- after I wrote the letter-- to have heard from him.
I don't know if maybe he didn't live there anymore.
Maybe he didn't survive himself.
But anyway, end of the war, we were working, as I said, in fields.
And one day, the head of the Gestapo in our camp-- we called him [NON-ENGLISH], that was [NON-ENGLISH].
He came into the field and he called-- there were seven girls my age.
We were the youngest.
He called us and gathered us.
And he told us, [NON-ENGLISH].
Come with me.
And he took us away.
And my sisters were working in the field that day, were watching as I was being led away.
And he was a terribly brutal man.
If a girl would do anything, he could just throw her on the floor, step on her neck, choke her.
He was a beast.
And the girls looked at us.
What are they going to do with those children?
Where are they taking them?
My sister started to cry.
And he led us to a different field and then he told us to run.
And as we started running, the seven of us, we thought, he's going to shoot us.
And, suddenly, he told us, halt.
And just like nothing, he told us, I want you to pick the flowers in the field.
First we thought, we don't hear right, or we are crazy, or is he crazy?
We picked the flowers, and we brought it to him.
And he started laughing at us because he saw that fear.
We did not know what he was doing to us.
And then he chased us in the field some more.
He made us run like-- as though we were horses and he was just chasing us.
And then, like this it went on for several hours.
Then he gathered us together-- the seven of us-- and brought us back.
Why he did it?
For his amusement again, which many times things were done to us by the Nazis for their amusement.
But what did they do to us?
Can you imagine?
When I look back and I saw my sister standing and I was being led away, I never thought that time that I will see them again.
It was again things to instill fear in us, and to terrorize us.
And these things were done to us by what we call human beings.
I wonder until today, how does a man do that to another human being?
How does a soldier pick up an infant, toss him in the air, use him as a target practice, come home and pick up his own child and not feel any pain and regrets?
I know that this concentration camp life in the ghetto has affected me in such a terrible way that I thought that I will never recover from it, that i will never be a normal human being.
Being exposed to so much brutality, being exposed to such a hunger, how could I ever turn the clock back and become a decent human being?
When we were working in the field, it was a terrible time because many girls froze, became very ill.
And we-- of course, we're being starved to death.
Some girls that were planting potatoes started eating them and became very, very ill.
On May 8, unknown to us, the war has officially ended.
But, that night, we went to sleep as usually, not knowing that we were being freed.
Only to wake up next morning, on May 9, to discover that there were no Nazi guards around.
There were no calls for work, no beatings, no whistle for work, nothing.
It was completely still.
It was so frightening to us.
Anything that was out of the ordinary frightened us.
We didn't know what's happening.
We are afraid to move.
But, soon, the girl begin moving about.
And one of the girls approached the gate and pushed it open.
We realized then that the war has ended.
I cannot describe to you the feeling.
All the girls ran towards the gate, ran into the street.
We're soon- we were approached by the oncoming Russian army.
Liberation Day was the greatest day in my life.
I shall also never forget it.
Here were Russian-- we were jumping on top of the tanks, on top of their trucks.
Kissing them, loving them, hugging them.
They were our liberators.
We didn't know then that this was already after the war has ended.
We thought that was our liberation.
But that evening, that first evening, we celebrated our liberation in the assembly hall of the factory.
By we, I mean, we, the prisoners from the concentration camp, also the POWs from the nearby camps.
And hear were people of different nationalities, different religions, and we were celebrating it as one people, filled with hope.
Yes, they did.
From the-- although, they did not have much themselves.
Just the bare necessities, bread.
Nothing much.
They themselves did not have much.
And you know, that first day, we felt like we are all one people.
And, immediately, I went to recover something that I have hidden when I came to camp.
You see, when I came to camp I was allowed to bring my things, but, soon, they were taken away from us.
But I had a few pictures, a little album, with pictures of my girlfriends.
And when we had to turn everything in, I decided that we should hide it someplace.
And we went to the place-- to the toilet on that hill.
And above the toilet, there was like a board, just a board.
I suppose it was to support it.
And we threw all those-- my little pictures on top of there.
Hoping that if we survive, and the pictures will survive, we will recover them.
We went to the hill, on the hill to the toilet.
We call the toilets-- we called it [NON-ENGLISH], which meant the holy mount.
We gave it a name.
I don't know why.
And we recovered the few pictures and I have them until today.
Those are school pictures.
That little locket, I hid it under the bed, you know, where the foot of the bed was resting.
I just pushed it there, you know, into a little hole, and that's it.
I couldn't hang onto it all the time and hold it in my mouth forever.
That was just for that temporary one day, then it was like that.
And I hid it and I have it until today.
It was not easy.
And that I had at least-- that I was clever enough that time to keep this and not to turn it in.
Because I was frightened to death there.
But, somehow, I took a chance, though I didn't realize that if they would find it on me, I could have been killed for it too.
But you're not always reason when you're faced with something like that.
Well, the Russians, of course, liberated us.
They couldn't help us, but they opened a warehouse where there were clothes from-- I don't know from where those clothes came.
And they told us that we could take if we find something.
And we did find-- I found a shirt and-- I don't remember if I hit a skirt, or slacks, or whatever.
And I took off the uniform.
And you know what it meant.
The uniform represented something that horrible.
And I took off the uniform, threw it off, and put on a shirt, a shirt that people wear.
You know what the piece of cloth meant to me that time?
It was-- well, it was the only possession I had.
It meant an awful lot.
And because the transportation of new world was paralyzed, we could not return home until two weeks later.
But while still in camp- and this I need to tell you-- the POWs from the near by camps and the girls begin mingling.
You know, boys, girls.
And one day, several of them walked to a nearby forest.
And in the forest, they discovered that my-- our [NON-ENGLISH], the Irma Hoffmann, the head of our concentration camp, was hiding in the forest.
She had a little suitcase with all our chains and everything that she was supposed to turn over, which she didn't.
Anyway, they brought her back to camp and we turned her over to the Russian authorities, who in turn told us, she is your prisoner.
Don't forget, the Russians, unlike, the Americans, did not hold Nuremberg trials.
Because, then, you must understand, they suffered by the Nazi hand much more than any other Allies.
And, therefore, they said, she's your prisoner.
Take her back to camp.
We give you the freedom to do with her whatever you choose.
You can kill her.
You can cut her to shreds.
She's yours.
So we brought her back to camp and took her to the cell appel, sat her on a chair.
And all of the girls, in whose heart I believed was enough hate for that woman, surrounded her.
But, you know, because we were exposed to inhumanity of man towards man, because we were exposed to death, because the way we were raised, I believe we still valued life too much.
And not one, not one of us could find the strength to go and kill her.
And, today, I am proud of the fact that we were not reduced to their level.
I'm also proud that we could not become killers.
It wouldn't have avenged our parents.
It wouldn't have given us the satisfaction.
We turned her over back to the Russian authorities and told them they have to deal with her.
All we did is shave her head and turn her back to the Russian authorities.
Today, I'm proud of the fact that we did not kill her, in spite of what she did to us.
